coredata.dc:title: Photostable yellow emissive carbon dots for iron-mediated reversible sensing of biothiols and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Shanshan
liu, Baoqiang
Cui, Dongfeng
Zhang, Hongyuan
Wang, Chenzhao
Yin, Xiangyu
Jiang, Chunzhu
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: The different levels of biothiols in cells can not only screen cancer cells but also play a selective role in killing cancer cells. Therefore, accurate monitoring of biothiol in cancer cells is of great research significance. Herein, novel yellow emission CDs (Y-CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using 2, 5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (DHTA) as precursors. The Y-CDs as a highly efficient dual-mode sensor could detect Fe3+ and biothiols by colorimetric and fluorescence signals. Especially, with the addition of L-Cysteine, the quenched fluorescence could be quickly restored within 2 min and the detection limit was as low as 31.65 nM. Additionally, this sensor was utilized to sense biothiols in actual samples and living cells due to its eminent biocompatibility. Finally, the Y-CDs were successfully applied not only as fluorescent ink for message encryption but also as a portable solid hydrogels sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and biothiols. Therefore, these results suggested that Y-CDs could serve as a promising sensor for Fe3+ and biothiol detection in early cancer screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent
Colorimetric
Sensor
Fe3+
Biothiol
Living cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of Y-CDs in biological level
Application of Y-CDs for fluorescent ink and message encryption
Application of Y-CDs for the solid sensor to recognize Fe3+ and L-Cys/GSH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation ( 20220101350JC ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U2241287 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HR-TEM, (b) size distribution, (c) EDS spectrum, (d) XRD and (e) FT-IR spectra of Y-CDs (f) XPS survey and high-resolution XPS of (g) C 1 s (h) N 1 s and (i) O 1 s spectra of Y-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black), excitation (pink) and emission (blue) spectra, the inset: photos of Y-CDs under sunlight (left) and UV light (right), (b) emission spectra under different excitation wavelength, (c) 3D fluorescence emission spectroscopy and (d) CIE spectroscopy of Y-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) the selectivity of Y-CDs and (b) Y-CDs + Fe3+ in fluorescent mode, (c) the selectivity of Y-CDs and (d) Y-CDs + Fe3+ in colorimetric mode.

(a) the fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs upon the addition of Fe3+, inset: fluorescence photos of Y-CDs + Fe3+, (b) a linear correction model (c) the UV–vis absorption spectra of Y-CDs in the presence of Fe3+; inset: the photos of Y-CDs + Fe3+ under the sun light (d) Linear plot of UV–vis absorbance versus the various concentration of Fe3+, (e) the fluorescence spectra of Y-CDs-Fe3+ in the presence of L-Cys, inset: the photos of of Y-CDs + Fe3+ +L-Cys (f) the line relationship between the concentrations of L-Cys and the value of (F-F0)/F (g) the UV–vis absorption spectra of Y-CDs + Fe3++L-Cys, inset: the photos of Y-CDs + Fe3+ +L-Cys under the sun light (h) Linear plot of UV–vis absorbance versus the various concentration of L-Cys, (i) the response time of Y-CDs + Fe3+ in presence and absence of L-Cys.

(a) the fluorescence lifetimes, (b) the line relation between F0/F and concentration of Fe3+, (c) the absorption spectra (Fe3+), excitation and emission spectra of Y-CDs (d) the absorption spectra of Fe3+, Y-CDs, Y-CDs + Fe3+, Y-CDs + Fe3++L-Cys, Y-CDs + Fe3++L-Hcy and Y-CDs + Fe3++GSH (e) TEM image and of Y-CDs + Fe3+ and Y-CDs + Fe3++L-Cys (f) the zeta potentials (g) the high resolution XPS spectra of O1s (g) Fe2p and (h) S2p.

(a) Cell viability, (b) images of 4 T1 cells co-incubated with Y-CDs for different concentrations and (c) different incubation times, (d) images of Y-CDs, Y-CDs + Fe3+ and Y-CDs + Fe3++L-Cys in bright field (a,b,c), blue channel (d,e,f) and merged (g,h,i), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Information writing, (b) Y-CDs as fluorescent ink for painting (a-j: sunlight; a1-j1: UV-light at 365 nm), (c) Fluorescent colors of the hydrogel solid sensor to recognize Fe3+ and L-Cys/GSH, respectively, (d) Photos of the solid sensor under sunlight after reacting for 10 min and 30 min (a1-d1 and a3-d3: Y-CDs,Y-CDs + Fe3+,Y-CDs + Fe3++L-Cys and Y-CDs + Fe3++GSH) and photos of the hydrogel solid sensor under UV light at 365 nm, respectively, (e) Digital photo of Fe3+ adsorption and desorption process.
Y-CDs not only as a multi-mode probe for the detection of Fe3+ and biothiols in various media.
Previously reported methods of Y-CDs + Fe3+ system for L-Cys detection.

head.ce:title: Photostable yellow emissive carbon dots for iron-mediated reversible sensing of biothiols and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The different levels of biothiols in cells can not only screen cancer cells but also play a selective role in killing cancer cells. Therefore, accurate monitoring of biothiol in cancer cells is of great research significance. Herein, novel yellow emission CDs (Y-CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using 2, 5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (DHTA) as precursors. The Y-CDs as a highly efficient dual-mode sensor could detect Fe3+ and biothiols by colorimetric and fluorescence signals. Especially, with the addition of L-Cysteine, the quenched fluorescence could be quickly restored within 2 min and the detection limit was as low as 31.65 nM. Additionally, this sensor was utilized to sense biothiols in actual samples and living cells due to its eminent biocompatibility. Finally, the Y-CDs were successfully applied not only as fluorescent ink for message encryption but also as a portable solid hydrogels sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and biothiols. Therefore, these results suggested that Y-CDs could serve as a promising sensor for Fe3+ and biothiol detection in early cancer screening.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomolecular thiols such as glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy) and cysteine (Cys) play a versatile role in physiological and pathological processes [1,2]. GSH is one of the most important and abundant nonprotein biothiols in cells [3]. It is reported that the level of endogenous GSH in many cancer cells is higher than that in normal tissues [4]. Therefore, the GSH can be applied as an important biomarker for tumor cells [3]. Cys as an essential amino acid maintains intracellular redox balance and protects cells from oxidative stress through reversible disulfide formation [5,6]. Abnormal levels of Cys are also associated with a variety of diseases [5,7,8]. Hcy is considered to be an independent marker of chronic metabolic disease [1,6]. Nevertheless, the effective distinguishing of the three biothiols has rarely been reported due to their similar structural and chemical properties [5,6,9]. Therefore, the development of a biosensor for the effective discrimination of the three biothiols in organisms is of great research interest [5,10].

Owing to the medicinal importance of biothiols, many classical detection methods already exist, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], UV–vis absorption spectrophotometry [6], electrochemical analysis and chemical luminescence method [12,13]. However, there is almost no way to effectively distinguish the three biological biothiols, which still faces serious challenges. As a comparison, the fluorescent probe has attracted great attention in detection and cells imaging because of the less damage, high sensitivity, fast response, easy preparation, real-time and continuous detection, which is considered as a promising platform for target recognition and analysis [4,9,10,14]. Meanwhile, colorimetric methods are also popular because of their simplicity, visualization, and reliability [15,16]. Consequently, the establishment of a multimode sensor to quickly identify biothiols in aqueous solution and living cells is an urgent problem in disease diagnosis [7,10].

Carbon dot (CDs) as a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial has acquired the great attention owing to their simple synthesis, easy surface modification, excellent photostability and biocompatibility [2,10,15,17,18]. Therefore, scientists have prepared a series of CDs with excellent properties using hydrothermal method, microwave assisted method, and electrochemical oxidation method etc [19,20]. Among them, hydrothermal method has the advantages of high particle purity, environmentally friendly, good dispersion and low production cost [21]. Therefore, hydrothermal syntheses of CDs have been successfully applied in various aspects such as biosensing, optoelectronic devices, cell imaging and catalysis [10,14,18,22,23]. Particularly, the breakthrough of CDs in biothiol sensing is expected to have clinic application potential in chemistry and biomarkers [24]. For instance, Deng and his colleagues prepared a sensitive nanosensor based on N-CDs/AuNPs that responds to L-Cys through three modes at the same time [7]. Yang and his colleges synthesized a dual-emission Hg2+-mediated radiometric fluorescent CDs for GSH sensing and in vivo imaging [4]. Moreover, Li and his colleagues designed nitrogen-doped CDs, which can not only detect Cu2+ and L-Cys in fluorescence mode but also as a fluorescent ink for multiple message encryption [17]. Unfortunately, researchers need to solve the problems of long response time, complex decoration, and high background interference [15,16,20,25]. In a word, it is imperative to establish a scientific, sensitive and practical method for biothiols detection.

In this work, a novel yellow emission carbon dots (Y-CDs) were synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal route using DHTA and urea as a precursor (Scheme 1 ). These Y-CDs as fluorescent/colorimetric sensor displayed remarkable sensitivity differences in response to Cys/GSH/Hcy at different times. Furthermore, the coordination between the hydroxyl group of Y-CDs and Fe3+ formed a dark blue complex, which induced the fluorescence quenching. Afterward, the fluorescence was effectively recovered after the addition of biothiol. Subsequently, the Y-CDs could serve as fluorescent ink for information encryption and decryption, as well as for multimodal detection of analytes, including hydrogels and cancer cells. Therefore, this strategy provides a new technique for the identification of biothiol molecules.

Briefly, 0.20 g DHTA, 0.10 g urea and 15 mL deionized water were placed in a Teflon lined autoclave and then transferred to an oven for continuous carbonization at 200 °C for 6 h. Thereafter, the yellow primary product was cooled to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, unreacted substances were removed by continuous dialysis with ultra-pure water (MW = 2000 Da) for 24 h under the condition of magnetic agitation. Eventually, the Y-CDs solution was filtered (0.22 μm) and lyophilized for further use.

Typically, the 0.10 M of Fe3+ solution was obtained by dissolving 0.162 g FeCl3. Then, 100 μL Fe3+ solutions (0–200 μM) was introduced into 200 μL Y-CDs solution and diluted to 3 mL with deionized water. Lastly, the mixed solution was fully reacted in a micro shaker for 5 min. For the detection of biothiols, various concentrations of GSH/L-Cys/L-Hcy were introduced into Y-CDs-Fe3+ system (200 μM of Fe3+), respectively. The following procedure was consistent with the Fe3+. Finally, all data were collected in both fluorescence and colorimetric modes.

Firstly, 0.05 g Y-CDs and 5 g agarose were dissolved in a 250 mL beaker and stirred evenly. Then, the solution was magnetically stirred for 8 min at 90 °C in the water bath. Finally, the product was poured into the mold and cooled to room temperature to form the solid hydrogel sensor.

Firstly, the reaction conditions including molar ratio, temperature, reaction time and solvent were optimized [20,26,27]. Specifically, the fluorescence intensities of the Y-CDs reached the maximum when DHTA (0.20 g) and urea (0.10 g) was mixed with pure water (15 mL) for reaction at 200 ◦C for 6 h (Fig. S1a-1d) [13,21]. Then, the microscopic morphology and structural composition of Y-CDs were observed and characterized by a variety of instruments [7,19,28]. As displayed in Fig. 1 a and 1b, the Y-CDs were primarily spherical and the average diameter was 8.13 ± 0.25 nm. The HR-TEM results suggested that Y-CDs were amorphous (inset in Fig. 1a) [10,16]. Likewise, the image of SAED illustrated that the Y-CDs did not contain crystal lattice (Fig. 1c) [15,29]. Furthermore, a diffraction peak at approximately 22.3° was obtained from Fig. 1d, further confirming the amorphous structure of Y-CDs [20]. The Raman spectrum of Y-CDs possessed two typical absorption bands at 1370 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 (Fig. S2), corresponding to the carbon atom vibrations with dangling bonds at the planar ends of disordered graphite and the two-dimensional vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms [19,30]. Additionally, the functional groups information of the Y-CDs was surveyed by FT-IR spectrum [31]. As displayed in Fig. 1e, the two absorption bands at 3434 cm−1 and 3120 cm−1 presented the stretching vibration of NH and OH, respectively [2,10]. There were two peaks at 1680 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC [25,29]. Moreover, the stretching vibrations of CC, CN and C-O appeared at 1431 cm−1, 1320 cm−1 and 1103 cm−1, respectively [18,31]. Collectively, these results demonstrated that there were large number of hydroxyl and ammine groups on the surface of Y-CDs, which ensured the excellent hydrophilicity of these Y-CDs [16,19,31].

The structural composition of Y-CDs was further explored by XPS [17,19]. Three main binding energies peaks located at 284.60 eV, 399.30 eV and 531.90 eV in the XPS full scan spectrum corresponded to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. 1f) [30]. The C1s were deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.50 eV (CC/CC), 286.20 eV (CO/CN) and 288.70 eV (CO), respectively (Fig. 1g) [32]. The N 1 s spectrum displayed two peaks at 399.20 eV and 400.80 eV, matching NH and CN bonds (Fig. 1h) [18,29,31]. Moreover, the O 1 s indicated the presence of CO and CO at 531.80 eV and 533.20 eV (Fig. 1i) [33]. The XPS date suggested that N element was successfully doped into the Y-CDs [15,17].

The optical feature of Y-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra [15,33]. There were two distinct absorption peaks in the UV–vis absorption spectrum at 245 nm and 350 nm, which were due to the π-π* transitions of CC and n-π* transitions of CO, respectively (Fig. 2 a) [4,7,28]. Moreover, the picture of the Y-CDs solution displayed yellow fluorescence under the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (inset in Fig. 2a). Then, the emission spectrum and 3D fluorescence spectrum in Fig. 2b and 2c illustrated that the strongest emission located at 530 nm when the optimum excitation wavelength at 350 nm (QY = 8.02%) [34]. Interestingly, the Y-CDs possessed typical excitation-independent phenomenon, which was usually attributed to the uniform surface states and small size [29,32]. Next, the fluorescence decay curve was fitted with three exponentials decay function and the fluorescence lifetime was 1.36 ns (Fig. 5a) [18]. Lastly, the chromaticity of the Y-CDs component could be determined using the CIE 1931 diagram. The CIE coordinate of Y-CDs was (0.27, 0.29), indicating extraordinary color purity of Y-CDs in the yellow region (Fig. 2d) [35].

The stability of the Y-CDs in complex biological environment was investigated [29]. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged with diverse temperature, concentrations of NaCl, H2O2 and UV irradiation time (Fig. S3a-S3c and S3e). And the Y-CDs also possessed excellent fluorescence stability in different solvents such as CP, EA, DMF, H2O, FA, EAC and MT (Fig. S3f) [19]. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs was measured after co-incubation with pH buffer solution (pH = 1–14) [2]. The fluorescence intensity fluctuated slightly in the pH range of 4–10 and was strongest under neutral conditions (pH = 7) (Fig. S3d). The phenomenon of pH dependence was attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of Y-CDs probably due to the presence of nitrogen atoms at the Y-CDs [15,32]. Besides, the stability of Y-CDs-Fe3+ system was also evaluated under different condition including the NaCl, H2O2, UV irradiation, pH, temperature and solvent, respectively. Similar results were obtained from the Y-CDs + Fe3+ system (S4a-S4f), which indicated that Y-CDs possessed excellent photostability and could be applied to detect analytes in complex biological samples [10,20,36].

To investigate the selectivity of Y-CDs towards Fe3+, the fluorescence response experiment in the presence of ions (Li+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Gd3+ and Fe3+) was explored [6,12]. Obviously, only Fe3+ could induce a strong fluorescent quenching at the emission wavelength of 530 nm (Fig. 3 a), while the influence of others could be ignored, indicating that the above mentioned metal ions had no great influence on the Fe3+ detection [37]. The competitive selectivity of Y-CDs + Fe3+ system towards multiple biological small molecules (L-Cys, L-Hcy, L-His, L-Phe, L-Lle, L-Asp, L-Glu, Ach and L-Try, GSH, CA and Glu) was also evaluated, respectively [24]. The addition of these interfering substances did not induce any significant fluorescence recovery except for biothiols (Fig. 3b). Subsequently, the selectivity of Y-CDs in colorimetric mode was further investigated (Fig. 3c and 3d), and the results demonstrated that Y-CDs had excellent colorimetric selectivity for Fe3+ and biothiols, [19,33,37]. Therefore, Y-CDs + Fe3+ system as a dual modes sensor could selectively detect biological biothiols.

For the quantitative detection of Fe3+ and L-Cys/L-Hcy/GSH, an “ON-OFF-ON” sensing platform based on Y-CDs was established [29,38]. Initially, the Y-CDs (21 μg mL−1) sensing capability for Fe3+ was investigated at pH of 7.0 under 350 nm [6]. The fluorescence intensity was quenched by 80 % after 4 min with the increase of Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 4 a and 4i). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs gradually descends with the introduction of Fe3+ under 365 UV lamps (Fig. 4a) [25]. The correction model ((F0-F)/F0 = 0.002x + 0.209) was established between (F0-F)/F0 and concentration from 20 to 200 μM (R2 = 0.990) (Fig. 4b), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 28.46 nM on the base of 3σ/s (σ is the strand deviation of the control, and s represents the slope of strand curve) [28,36,37]. Meanwhile, the response signal of Y-CDs to Fe3+ in UV–vis channel was investigated [6]. The absorption of Y-CDs enhanced gradually when the solution color altered from colorless transparent to blue under daylight (Fig. 4c). An excellent linear relationship (10–80 μM) was fitted with the LOD of 0.68 μM (Fig. 4d) [4]. Therefore, these experiments results demonstrated that the Y-CDs could be employed for detecting Fe3+ by fluorescence/colorimetric mode [15,16,31].

The feasibility of Y-CDs + Fe3+ system for L-Cys detection was further investigated by fluorescent and colorimetric methods [12,38]. The fluorescence recovery ability of Y-CDs + Fe3+ was recorded in the addition of L-Cys [5,7]. The fluorescence signal at 530 nm was recovered through 2 min when the concentration of the L-Cys was 150 μM (Fig. 4e and 4i) [7,29]. And the maximum recover efficiency arrived 100 %. Fig. 4c displayed an excellent linear relation between the L-Cys in the interval 10–100 μM and the (F-F0)/F, the LOD was 31.65 nM, which possessed great advantages over many reported in the literature (Table 1 ). Moreover, fluorescence quenching and recovery experiments were performed with Fe3+ and L-Cys solutions at pH 7.0. After 10 cycles, the fluorescence of Y-CDs was restored in the presence of L-Cys, which suggested that the Y-CDs had excellent recycling characteristics and possessed great potential for practical application (Fig. S8a) [15,18,38]. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption peak at 210 nm declined in the presence of L-Cys and the color of the solution faded (inset in Fig. 4g). The LOD was calculated as 0.74 μM with R2 = 0.997 (Fig. 4h). Therefore, Y-CDs as a convenient and intuitive fluorescent/colorimetric sensor to detect L-Cys in water solution [30,38].

Subsequently, the sensing performance of Y-CDs + Fe3+ system towards L-Hcy was evaluated by fluorescence spectroscopy under 530 nm emission [6,13]. As described in Fig. S5a and S5b, the recovery results of Y-CDs reached 80 % when the L-Hcy concentration was 2.0 mM after 10 min (Fig. S7a), suggesting that the Y-CDs + Fe3+ sensor possessed the ability to sensitively detect L-Hcy [5,12]. And the LOD was 0.13 μM with the R2 = 0.995. Subsequently, the blue solution of Y-CDs + Fe3+ transform to colorless when the L-Hcy was introduced (Fig. S5c and inset in Fig. S5d). Then, the concentration of L-Hcy and the (A-A0)/A value could be fitted into a perfect linear (Fig. S5d), and the LOD was 14.10 μM with the R2 = 0.995. Therefore, Y-CDs possessed significant selectivity for L-Hcy determination [19,35].

Lastly, the detection performance was evaluated by adding incremental concentration of GSH into Y-CDs + Fe3+ system [4,29]. Fig. S6a and S7b displayed that the fluorescence restored after 150 μM GSH was introduced for 5 min. Interestingly, the fluorescence emission blue shifted to 515 nm and the fluorescence color rapidly changed from yellow to green compared with the Y-CDs when GSH was added (inset in Fig. S6a). Additionally, the fitting equation could be expressed as y = 0.0093x + 0.0417, R2 = 0.993 (Fig. S6b) and the LOD was 42.79 nM. The cyclic experiment could also be performed correctly 10 times, demonstrating that the Y-CDs as a sensor for cyclic detection of Fe3+ and GSH (Fig. S8b) [18,33]. Moreover, the colorimetric signal was measured by UV–vis spectra. The influence of color and absorption peak intensity gradually declined with the enhance of GSH concentration (inset in Fig. S6c) [21,32]. Importantly, the linear plot of UV–vis absorbance at 210 nm the various concentration of GSH could be obtained (y = 0.00701x-0.0334, R2 = 0.996) (Fig. S6c and S6d), and the LOD was 0.43 μM. In summary, Y-CDs + Fe3+ as an effective fluorescence probe to track GSH in biological systems by dual modes, which provided an advanced technique for the rapid identification of biothiols [19,28,33].

The mechanism of Y-CDs sensing Fe3+ and biothiols was investigated [9,17]. The fluorescence quenching progress of Y-CDs towards Fe3+ was investigated via the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1) F0/F = 1 + kSV [Fe3+] = 1 + Kqτ0

K sv is the Stern-Volmer constant, K q is the molecular quenching rate constant and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of Y-CDs without analyte. The Kq was computed as 3.2 × 1011 L·mol−1·s−1 according to Fig. 5 b, demonstrating that the static quenching occurred [16,28,29]. The fluorescence attenuation curve of Y-CDs declined from 1.36 ns to 1.01 ns in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 5a), which further confirmed that static quenching happened [36]. Moreover, a new absorption peak appeared between 450 and 780 nm when Fe3+ was introduced, illustrating that a ground-state non-luminous complex formed (Fig. 5d) [39]. Subsequently, the XPS spectra further confirmed the formation of complexes between Fe3+ and Y-CDs [3,34]. There was a significant decrease in O1s when Fe3+ ions added to the system, indicating that electrons of N and O elements participated in the coordination of Fe3+, and carboxyl and amino groups became active sites involved in the recognition of Fe3+ (Fig. 5g) [17]. Meanwhile, the Fe2p could be divided into two peaks Fe2p3/2 (711.01 eV), Fe2p1/2 (724.62 eV), and a pair of satellite satellite peak at 715.36 eV and 732.06 eV, respectively (Fig. 5h) [40]. Therefore, the above data indicated that it was reasonable for Y-CDs to form coordination complexes with Fe3+ [17,25,40].

Moreover, as described in Fig. 5c, the excitation peak of Y-CDs at 350 nm overlapped the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ in a wide range, which suggested that the internal filtering effect (IFE) happened [21]. Therefore, the IFE was investigated by the Parker equation.
(2)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em

- s
A
em
(3)
E
obsd
= 1 -
F
obsd
F
obsd , 0
(4)
E
cor
= 1 -
F
cor
F
cor , 0

Here, Aex, Aem, Fobsd and Fcor represent the absorbance per centimeter at the Ex of 350 nm corresponding to the Em of 530 nm, and the measured and corrected fluorescence intensities. Eobsd and Ecor represent the efficiency of fluorescence intensity change after measurement and correction, respectively [28]. Therefore, the IFE effect enhanced with the increasing of the value of Fco/Fob (1.01 to 1.76), which was matched with the Parker equation. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Y-CDs by Fe3+ could be attributed to the effects of static quenching and IFE [16,18,39].

Subsequently, the recovery progress of Y-CDs was investigated after introducing biothiols [18,33]. Take L-Cys as an example, the Zeta potential value of Y-CDs was −9.82 mV (Fig. 5f). When Fe3+ irons were introduced, it increased to 0.41 mV because Fe3+ could successfully bind to Y-CDs and consumed hydroxyl groups or other negative ions on those surfaces [4,10,29]. Subsequently, the Zeta potential value decreased to −6.28 mV after the addition of L-Cys, resulting in fluorescence recovery. Moreover, the TEM photos of the Y-CDs-Fe3+ system before and after the addition of L-Cys were collected to explain the quenching and recovery possess [4,6,10]. The size of Y-CDs + Fe3+ system enlarged to 50.23 nm and recovered to about 20.05 nm after the introduction of L-Cys (Fig. 5e). While the addition of L-Cys could dissolve the aggregation and the solution color changed colorless, inducing to the fluorescence recovery [4,29]. Lastly, the fluorescence recover process was investigated via the XPS spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 5i, the high-resolution S2p spectrum displayed three components including Fe-S (162.02 eV), C-S-C 2p3/2 (163.43 eV) and C-S-C 2p1/2 (164.65 eV) [40]. The presence of Fe-S peaks indicated that the Y-CDs + Fe3+ complex dissociated due to the strong coordination of L-Cys with Fe3+, resulting that the fluorescence was effectively restored [17,39].

The feasibility of this research strategy was explored in Tap water and BSA samples, respectively [34]. The T-test demonstrated that there was no significant difference between the standard curve method and the working curve method at the 95 % confidence level [28,34]. Therefore, the standard curve method was applied to analyze the actual sample. As illustrated in Table S1-S4 the dual-mode assay had satisfactory recoveries in range from 83.03 % to 100.96 % with RSD values of less than 4 % for three average tests (Fig. S9-S10). Therefore, the above research results indicated that this strategy presents a feasible method for further detection of Fe3+ and biothiols in actual tap water and BSA samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Y-CDs in biological level
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the intracellular applicability of Y-CDs, their toxicity was evaluated by CCK8 assay using 4 T1 cells line [10,21,37,39,41]. The survival rate of 4 T1 cells was more than 92 % after incubation with 800 μg mL−1 for 24 h (Fig. 6 a). Therefore, the outstanding biocompatibility would endow these Y-CDs with great potential in image of living cell [24,29,31,42]. Thereafter, the imaging ability of the Y-CDs was further investigated by inverted fluorescence microscopy. As displayed in Fig. 6b and 6c, the remarkable green fluorescence in the cell cytoplasm was observed in the 4 T1 cells when the concentration of Y-CDs was 150 μg mL−1 after incubation for 5 h under the blue channel excitation of inverted fluorescence microscope (Fig. 6b). Subsequently, exogenous Fe3+ was introduced into the cells to further investigate the application of Y-CDs in semi-quantitative monitoring of the intracellular Fe3+ level. Satisfactorily, the strong green fluorescence darkened with the addition of Fe3+ (20 μM, 10 min), suggesting these Y-CDs could react with Fe3+ to produce discernible fluorescence signal in living cells (Fig. 6d) [13,28,39]. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity recovered after the introduction of L-Cys. Consequently, the Y-CDs as a biocompatible sensor possessed the potential to detect Fe3+ and biothiols in living cells [10,21,28,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Y-CDs for fluorescent ink and message encryption
ce:section.ce:para: At present, fluorescein, organic dyes, QDs, and CDs could all be applied in fields such as the image painting and message encryption [25,43]. Therefore, the aqueous solution of Y-CDs was filled into an empty pen to write the fluorescent character and to draw fluorescent pictures. Particularly, it could be observed that a clear pattern of yellow fluorescence was displayed under ultraviolet irradiation, and the outline and color were obvious (Fig. 7 b) [17]. In other hand, the multiple encryption and decryption of information was an interesting research direction. The “on–off-on” Y-CDs were synthesized and the multi-encryption of information was realized by using fluorescence technology [17,32,43]. Firstly, the encrypted information (digital (Fig. 7a, a1-e1), English (a2-e2) and Chinese (a3-e3) were written on the filter paper with Y-CDs fluorescent ink, which could not be observed under daylight. The encrypted contents were decrypted for the first time under an ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm. Then, the filter paper was placed to the beaker containing Fe3+ solution for 1 min. No information was observed under the sunlight and UV lamp, demonstrating that the encryption of information of Y-CDs-Fe3+ system could be achieved [17]. Finally, the filter paper containing Y-CDs-Fe3+ was added to the test tube containing two biological and placed for different time, respectively. The fluorescence of the Y-CDs could be restored and visible under UV lamp at 365 nm, which indicated that the system could complete the decryption of information [19,25]. Therefore, Y-CDs could be applied as fluorescent ink for writing and painting as well as could be utilized for the purpose of information storage and encryption and other commercial fields due to their special fluorescent properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Y-CDs for the solid sensor to recognize Fe3+ and L-Cys/GSH
ce:section.ce:para: A portable solid hydrogel sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and biomercaptan was developed to meet the portable requirements [17]. Agarose was selected as the carrier of fluorescent matrix because of its advantages such as controllable shape, rapid gel formation and no fluorescent background interference [39]. The fluorescence color of the hydrogel detection kit decreased when the Fe3+ were added (0–300 μM) (Fig. 7c). Meanwhile, a color transformed from colorless to blue under daylight. Importantly, the detection timeliness was also crucial for field detection evaluation. Therefore, the color change of the sensor of water hydrogel with different reaction time was investigated [17]. The color change was visible when the concentration of Fe3+ was equal to or greater than 70 μM for a reaction time of 10 min (Fig. 7c(c) and (d)). Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the kit increased after addition of biothiol and the blue color visible to the naked eye in daylight gradually faded to colorless (Fig. 7c(e)-(h)) [18]. Moreover, the elastic lattice structure of the hydrogel provided a spatial structure for Y-CDs, which realized the tracking and absorption of Fe3+ and biothiol in aqueous solution by three-dimensional materials (Fig. 7d: inset: a1-d4). The Y-CDs hydrogels had strong yellow fluorescence compared with the non-fluorescence of pure hydrogels, which could be significantly quenched within 10 min after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 7d (b1-b4)), and then recovered fluorescence within 10 min in the presence of L-Cys, while GSH required a longer time of 30 min (Fig. 7d1-7d4). Therefore, Y-CDs-based agarose-hydrogels had great potential in the visual detection field of biothiols in real samples due to their good portability and obvious color excess. Finally, the bear models were put into the solution containing Y-CDs,Y-CDs + Fe3+,Y-CDs + Fe3++L-Cys to observe the adsorption and desorption process and photos were taken after reaching the adsorption equilibrium, respectively (Fig. 7e).

In a word, the Y-CDs as an effective nanosensor could selectively detect Fe3+ and biothiols by fluorescent/colorimetric method and possessed a low LOD with a wide line range. Importantly, the accuracy of the proposed method was confirmed by measuring the content of Fe3+ and biothiols in Tap water and BSA samples, respectively. Moreover, the coordination between the hydroxyl group of Y-CDs and Fe3+ formed a dark blue complex, which induced the fluorescence quenching. Subsequently, the fluorescence was effectively recovered by the strong interaction between biothiol and Fe3+. Finally, Y-CDs could apply not only as a solid sensor to identify Fe3+ and biothiols but also as fluorescent ink for information encryption. Therefore, this work made an important contribution to the rational design of fast and accurate fluorescent probes in the field of clinical biomedicine to distinguish three kinds of biological thiols.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2241287).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123364.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
